notes: Central to the architectural dispute: IBM/EDS proposed centralized DB2 on mainframe in Tallahassee; all transactions routed through this single point. Reused from cache.
The proposed centralized architecture; IBM ES/9000 series mainframe family in use circa 1990-1992. Peak CPU hit 95-100% under production load. Reused from cache.
Welfare systems were COBOL-based; California EDD and Florida HRS both ran COBOL legacy systems. Reused from cache.
Peer-to-peer distributed architecture from the Burroughs side of Unisys; proposed as alternative to centralized mainframe; well-suited for regional processing.
Unisys distributed computing architecture from the Sperry UNIVAC lineage; peer-to-peer, regionally distributed; contrasted against IBM's centralized approach.
The emerging alternative to centralized mainframe; Unisys's architecture was aligned with distributed/client-server paradigm that was winning the 1990s platform wars. Reused from cache.
The architectural paradigm that Unisys championed and IBM/EDS rejected for the Florida HRS system; validated by subsequent system failures. Reused from cache.
Core workload type for welfare systems: caseworker queries checking eligibility across multiple programs; sub-second response time required. Reused from cache.

Combines Information Technology Management Reform Act (Cohen) + Federal Acquisition Reform Act (Clinger); enacted 1996; established CIO requirements, capital planning, performance-based IT management; still in force 2026.

IBM mainframe scale-out architecture developed partly in response to the performance limitations exposed by cases like Florida HRS; mentioned in research as IBM's answer to mainframe scalability critiques.
Government procurement relied almost exclusively on waterfall methods; requirements frozen before development began; catastrophic for multi-year projects in rapidly changing technology environments.
